[2023-09-30 19:29] kevinb9n: if there was a Drone with buildtime 5 would you pay even 1 gold for it?
[2023-09-30 19:32] kevinb9n: I ask because any time you choose Drone instead of Doomed Drone it seems to me like that's almost exactly what you'd be doing.  So it seems (to my novice mind) like doomed drone is pretty decent even if it never blocks.
[2023-09-30 19:38] awaclus: you'd pay it for sure if you'd otherwise float gold
[2023-09-30 19:39] awaclus: If it doesn't othereise screw up your build order
[2023-09-30 19:39] kevinb9n: if I come up $1 short on any of the next several turns I think I'd regret it, no?
[2023-09-30 19:39] kevinb9n: seems like a risk calculation
[2023-09-30 19:40] kevinb9n: but in any case, I'm with you - I'm certainly not actually arguing "always buy doomed over regular"
[2023-09-30 19:40] awaclus: If you come up $1 short like two turns later, at that point it's more like a bt3 drone for 1 and no energy
[2023-09-30 19:41] awaclus: Which is clearly better than a bt1 drone for 3 and energy
[2023-09-30 19:42] awaclus: But yeah, if doomed fits your build order better than drone, it's fine to get it even if you don't get block value
[2023-09-30 19:44] kevinb9n: the block is nice but I feel like the wiki oversells it? I get that paying $1 for 1 prompt soak is suuper efficient. But (a) that's only in the absolute best case where you have a way to put the soak to good use on the very last turn. Which your opponent can see coming a mile away and will try to deny you. And (b) the efficiency is great as a ratio but as you can't count on doing this with more than 1-2 DDs per turn, so it's not efficiency that "adds up" to a lot. And (c) at buy-time, that blocking bargain is so far off in the future that I feel like it should only raise what I was willing to pay by like 0.1.
[2023-09-30 19:53] kevinb9n: (because it seems to amount to, like, "if all goes perfectly you will save 1.5 money, 4 turns from now!" That surely seems worth less than 0.5 to me...)

(Base+5: Omega Splitter, Doomed Mech, Feral Hound, Wild Drone and Steel Forge)

so, this game, I'm not exactly sure how to learn from it, or what exactly I've done wrong, feedback would be greatly appreciated:

- I see that it is primarily super-blue focused (hence p1 favored), so I try to make up for that by gaining 3 drones by turn 2 (granted 2 of them are wild), as compared to p1 gaining 2 drones, hence my double wild drone start

-  Turn 4 was also a very interesting decision point, I didn't want to float 3 gold, so I ended up fitting in another wild drone and a conduit (both of which I might have regretted??), the conduit I thought might help me later on / make up for p1 advantage in blue sets, but either I misplayed it really badly or it wasn't so helpful

- I think the fourth omega splitter was too greedy, that much is clear, maybe the third one was too, at that point I needed more soak to defend. I thought I can get a lot of value with omega splitters and defend with forcefields (because I have green, while my opponent doesn't), but that just lead to me losing more and more drones, we were equal in attack but I was behind in drones the entire game

maybe doomed mech + steelsplitter is better than the second omega splitter?

on turn 4, maybe two blastforges?


also.. wild drone lets player 2 build turn 2 animus with a better economy than usual.  So I'm not even sure that focusing on blue is the only way to play it.

[2025-09-05 14:05] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Nvm, I played through all and wrote a story -- hope you like reading!
[2025-09-05 14:06] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): ## Unfair 1:
||If the end of scenario hint is anything to go by, there are likely various strategies/solutions to this. Here is mine:|| --> ||They have +1 engi. What is the main impact of this?|| --> ||The biggest impact of this is they have +1 energy, in other words able to boom in economy. The secondary, lesser impact is they have +1 defensive tempo. What to do?|| --> ||The +1 energy econ threat is dangerous, do not let them use it! ... Okay, but how do we accomplish that goal?|| --> ||Consider being aggressive early, to prevent them from making use of their energy advantage for economy. ... Godspeed!|| --> ||Wait, I am using Electrovores but still can't quite get there! Help! ... || --> ||If you are starting things well, likely you should find the AI spamming defense to try to survive your pressure. The main way in which you might still struggle is if they make efficient attack behind a bunch of defense. If they have a lot of piled up defense, more than your current threat, consider making Bloodrager to gain a long-term advantage. I think this is my last "strategy" tip, beyond this you also should play in a tactically sound manner, if you feel unsure regarding the tactical efficiency, I can help with that too, or also provide actual direct steps towards a solution. Godspeed!||
[2025-09-05 14:06] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): ## Unfair 2:
||They have +1 engi AND VIVID DRONE IS AVAILABLE?! So unfair... Hmm, what's good against or in the presence of Vivid drone?|| --> ||What about Savior? ... Interesting, maybe we can try that strategy. But wait, what's that? I SEE SOMETHING THAT IS VERY GOOD AGAINST VIVID DRONES! Do you see it?|| --> ||YEP, THAT'S RIGHT! APOLLO, BABY! If our opponent makes enough Vivid drones, and we get an Apollo up and manage to survive long enough, surely it's ggez. Can we make it happen? What do we need to able to do it, what are the weaknesses of Apollo?|| --> ||Well, it costs 3 blue, which is hefty. Especially if after buying an Apollo we cannot spend the blue. Also we need to be able to actually survive and not get rushed down. Are there other units in the set that help with these problems?|| --> ||As it turns out, yes there are! Infusion grid increases the absorb in the set (1 higher than wall), and gives us extra defense supply, also known as "soak" (40 more HP with 10 infusion grids ON TOP OF the 30 HP provided by Wall supply in base set) which may buy enough time for Apollo value over time to dominate. Also, Militia help us spend blue (blue sink).|| --> ||Okay, so maybe there is enough here to make Apollo work: great value over time by sniping Vivids if our opponent makes many; Infusion grid to defend while our Apollo value is ticking; Militia to help sink our excess blue and develop some attack of our own. Godspeed!||
[2025-09-05 14:06] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): ## Extremely Unfair: 
Phase 1: ||Initial Analysis||
||BRUH! They have ONE WHOLE EXTRA DRONE. SURELY this is impossible... (who's Shirley?) I mean doesn't this mean that they can do literally ANY kind of strategy just better than us?|| --> ||In theory, yes, of course. But remember, it's not a perfect opponent operating the other side -- the AI makes mistakes!|| --> ||Okay, but what mistakes, and when? Are there maybe certain situations where it tends to make more mistakes? How can we find out the answer to these questions?|| --> ||Well, let's think of our approach to this. Before even looking at any of the units in the set, let's decide how we are going to approach this problem. The quickest way to gain insight towards understanding the problem and working towards a solution is probably to start exploring the situation by playing the challenge and seeing what happens. Rewind a few turns if you feel like the opponent's response is too strong or if you made some tactical inaccuracies, and try to hone in on lines where you feel like you are making some progress.|| --> ||Based on that type of exploratory analysis, here is what I have found while playing around in this challenge today:|| --> ||First of all, let's decide our initial board state analysis before looking at the units in the set and before making any move on turn 1: they are P2 and they have 1 extra drone than usual -- 8 instead of 7. Usually P1 starts with 6 Drones (same as we do here), and P2 starts with 7. So, usually P1 has 86% (`6/7 = 0.857`) the # of Drones that P2 has, in exchange for going first. In this case however, we have 75% (`6/8 = 0.75`) the # of Drones of P2. Another way to think of it is that P2 has 14% (`8/7 = 1.14`) more Drones at the start than usual. Where am I going with this? Well... An interesting observation is that at higher Drone counts, 1 extra Drone is not as significant as at lower Drone counts. What can we do with that observation to guide our initial exploration direction?|| -->
[2025-09-05 14:07] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Phase 2: ||Plotting A Course (Exploration Direction)||
||Well, let's try to force a medium/high-econ game, rather than a low-econ one. After all, if we open with a quick aggro strategy and stay at low econ, we allow our opponent to keep a larger relative econ advantage (higher % drone advantage). So let's explore in a medium/high-econ direction, sound good? ... "WOAH STOP RIGHT THERE!" you might exclaim -- that +1 Drone they have surely means that they will beat us even in medium/high-econ situations. "Resistance is futile, let's just resign." Well... you are not wrong, but remember what we said at the start: our opponent is not perfect, far from it. By going for medium/high-econ, we reduce our relative econ disadvantage and open up more opportunities for our opponent to make mistakes -- perhaps enough mistakes that we may snatch victory from the jaws of defeat. Let's try.|| --> ||Okay, so we have a direction: medium/high-econ. Let's explore the higher econ direction first, after all, the more Drones on the board, the less our relative disadvantage is, right? ... Assuming our opponent mirrors a high econ strategy anyway. They might just not go high econ and try to rush us down. In that case, are there units in the set that support a defensive high econ strategy that can survive a rush? Or are the rush units too aggressive in the set? Let's now finally look at the units in the set and see...|| -->
[2025-09-05 14:07] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Phase 3: ||Reading The Set||
||As you become a stronger player, you will become better at set reading. Do not worry if you could not glean sufficiently valuable insights by simply looking at the set! Here is what I saw: plenty of great soak (Polywall, Aegis, Shredder, Chieftain); lack of fast aggro-type threat (the most dangerous are Feral and Cryo Ray, but neither on their own are very fast threats); some decent but not the best long-term attackers (Tantalum and Feral are both better than Tarsier, but not the greatest attackers in the game by any means); Manticore is not a strategy-definer, but is worth mentioning as a good blue macro enabler if you want to be on 2+ blue; Auric and Wild drone can help balance our econ and give greater room to posture/pivot during the early rounds ("build order" phase). ... What does this set read tell us?|| --> ||At first glance, with this set read, it seems there are no dangerous early game rush threats. While the absorb threshold is only base set (Wall, with 2 absorb), the lack of very fast and efficient attackers means that we can likely get away with a medium/high-econ approach. My instinct tells me that Feral + Tarsier rush can be scary though, so maybe we cannot get away with super high-econ -- we would need some better absorb than Wall, which is not available in this set. So maybe let's not go to extreme on high econ, try with a more moderate medium econ approach.|| -->
[2025-09-05 14:07] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Phase 4: ||Exploration (Playing The Challenge)||
||So, I set it with a 3 engi build, and got to turn 13 before I felt I fell too far behind. A key observation I learnt from this attempt is: ...|| --> ||The AI made a lot of early shredders in response to my 3rd engi. That is an interesting strategy that I overlooked in my initial set reading: Shredders are quite efficient attackers, and can be utilized as part of an effective aggro rush... but their Frontline makes them not viable in most scenarios. Can we use this observation to our advantage in any way?|| --> ||My thought at this point is: can we exploit the AI making early Shredders and get ahead by killing them?|| --> ||For my second attempt, I rewinded all the way to turn 2, and made some changes so that I can more efficiently build up attack while defending, focusing on killing their Shredders (which they went for again). It seemed good, but after killing all their Shredders and after the dust had settled, I had a bit too low econ (9 Drones). Not enough to even buy 1 Polywall per turn. Let's go back and see if we can do better! What should we change this time?|| --> ||Okay, what about not going for a 3 engi opener, and seeing if they still go for Shredders anyway? ... They only went for 1 Shredder on my next attempt. I reset at Turn 15. Onto the next attempt!|| --> ||I went back and saw that I bought a forge but floated the blue next turn due to my opponent's play. I explored delaying the forge. ... And... that's victory! In the follow spoilers I can give you a few details of what types of mistakes the AI made when I played this line, and can give you hints as to what my line was.|| -->
[2025-09-05 14:07] spiritfryer (Spyrfyr): Phase 5: ||Explaining My Solution||
||The AI made only 1 Shredder again, as in an earlier attempt, but this time after fixing our "too early Forge" inefficiency, we had enough tempo going that the AI just... stopped ever absorbing for some reason?! It had 1 blue and no Walls, and was making 1 Polywall per turn. Perhaps it figured that absorbing is bad because it had a number of frontline units (Wild Drones)? Either way, this is GREAT news for us, because we are up by +2 WHOLE ABSORB per turn. That is an insane advantage. Is it enough? ... Okay after a few turns the AI starts holding Steelsplitters, so they have absorb now, but with the tempo advantage we managed to buy out ALL 10 TARSIERS! At this point I had enough momentum to coast to victory, with a few minor tactical adjustments here or there.|| --> ||"Okay... but so what was the line?" you might ask. Well, as mentioned earlier I initially explored 3-engi openers, and then switched to a 2-engi opener. I observed it always opening with an early Forge on 2 engis, and that remained true throughout all my attempts today. In order to exploit Shredder-killing ASAP, I open with a turn 3 Animus, and make 1 Auric (+1 Drone) instead of 2 Drones, to keep my turns flexible.|| --> ||"What then?" Glad you asked. I pump Tarsiers whenever I can. In previous attempts I opened quick forge for a Wall, but as I mentioned, at some point that felt inefficient and I even floated an early blue. So the winning fix was to delay the forge by a turn, and use 1 early Rhino for defense in the meantime.|| --> ||"And?" Well, from there it was a matter of sharp tactical play, prioritizing Tarsiers while defending well, exploiting oppo defenses and minimizing their exploits on us. I had 12 Drones, often +1 Auric, and sometimes Wild Drones that the AI often killed very soon.|| --> ||I realize that this has been more of a diary than an explicit guide, but I hope you have found it helpful! Godspeed, and let me know if you still end up stuck!||

Also, Militia help us spend blue (blue sink).|| --> ||Okay, so maybe there is enough here to make Apollo work: great value over time by sniping Vivids if our opponent makes many; Infusion grid to defend while our Apollo value is ticking; Militia to help sink our excess blue and develop some attack of our own. Godspeed!||

messages.author: spiritfryer
messages.content:
## Extremely Unfair: 
Phase 1: ||Initial Analysis||
||BRUH! They have ONE WHOLE EXTRA DRONE. SURELY this is impossible... (who's Shirley?) I mean doesn't this mean that they can do literally ANY kind of strategy just better than us?|| --> ||In theory, yes, of course. But remember, it's not a perfect opponent operating the other side -- the AI makes mistakes!|| --> ||Okay, but what mistakes, and when? Are there maybe certain situations where it tends to make more mistakes? How can we find out the answer to these questions?|| --> ||Well, let's think of our approach to this. Before even looking at any of the units in the set, let's decide how we are going to approach this problem. The quickest way to gain insight towards understanding the problem and working towards a solution is probably to start exploring the situation by playing the challenge and seeing what happens. Rewind a few turns if you feel like the opponent's response is too strong or if you made some tactical inaccuracies, and try to hone in on lines where you feel like you are making some progress.|| --> ||Based on that type of exploratory analysis, here is what I have found while playing around in this challenge today:|| --> ||First of all, let's decide our initial board state analysis before looking at the units in the set and before making any move on turn 1: they are P2 and they have 1 extra drone than usual -- 8 instead of 7. Usually P1 starts with 6 Drones (same as we do here), and P2 starts with 7. So, usually P1 has 86% (`6/7 = 0.857`) the # of Drones that P2 has, in exchange for going first. In this case however, we have 75% (`6/8 = 0.75`) the # of Drones of P2. Another way to think of it is that P2 has 14% (`8/7 = 1.14`) more Drones at the start than usual. Where am I going with this? Well... An interesting observation is that at higher Drone counts, 1 extra Drone is not as significant as at lower Drone counts. What can we do with that observation to guide our initial exploration direction?|| -->

messages.author: spiritfryer
messages.content:
Phase 2: ||Plotting A Course (Exploration Direction)||
||Well, let's try to force a medium/high-econ game, rather than a low-econ one. After all, if we open with a quick aggro strategy and stay at low econ, we allow our opponent to keep a larger relative econ advantage (higher % drone advantage). So let's explore in a medium/high-econ direction, sound good? ... "WOAH STOP RIGHT THERE!" you might exclaim -- that +1 Drone they have surely means that they will beat us even in medium/high-econ situations. "Resistance is futile, let's just resign." Well... you are not wrong, but remember what we said at the start: our opponent is not perfect, far from it. By going for medium/high-econ, we reduce our relative econ disadvantage and open up more opportunities for our opponent to make mistakes -- perhaps enough mistakes that we may snatch victory from the jaws of defeat. Let's try.|| --> ||Okay, so we have a direction: medium/high-econ. Let's explore the higher econ direction first, after all, the more Drones on the board, the less our relative disadvantage is, right? ... Assuming our opponent mirrors a high econ strategy anyway. They might just not go high econ and try to rush us down. In that case, are there units in the set that support a defensive high econ strategy that can survive a rush? Or are the rush units too aggressive in the set? Let's now finally look at the units in the set and see...|| -->

messages.author: spiritfryer
messages.content:
Phase 3: ||Reading The Set||
||As you become a stronger player, you will become better at set reading. Do not worry if you could not glean sufficiently valuable insights by simply looking at the set! Here is what I saw: plenty of great soak (Polywall, Aegis, Shredder, Chieftain); lack of fast aggro-type threat (the most dangerous are Feral and Cryo Ray, but neither on their own are very fast threats); some decent but not the best long-term attackers (Tantalum and Feral are both better than Tarsier, but not the greatest attackers in the game by any means); Manticore is not a strategy-definer, but is worth mentioning as a good blue macro enabler if you want to be on 2+ blue; Auric and Wild drone can help balance our econ and give greater room to posture/pivot during the early rounds ("build order" phase). ... What does this set read tell us?|| --> ||At first glance, with this set read, it seems there are no dangerous early game rush threats. While the absorb threshold is only base set (Wall, with 2 absorb), the lack of very fast and efficient attackers means that we can likely get away with a medium/high-econ approach. My instinct tells me that Feral + Tarsier rush can be scary though, so maybe we cannot get away with super high-econ -- we would need some better absorb than Wall, which is not available in this set. So maybe let's not go to extreme on high econ, try with a more moderate medium econ approach.|| -->

messages.author: spiritfryer
messages.content:
Phase 4: ||Exploration (Playing The Challenge)||
||So, I set it with a 3 engi build, and got to turn 13 before I felt I fell too far behind. A key observation I learnt from this attempt is: ...|| --> ||The AI made a lot of early shredders in response to my 3rd engi. That is an interesting strategy that I overlooked in my initial set reading: Shredders are quite efficient attackers, and can be utilized as part of an effective aggro rush... but their Frontline makes them not viable in most scenarios. Can we use this observation to our advantage in any way?|| --> ||My thought at this point is: can we exploit the AI making early Shredders and get ahead by killing them?|| --> ||For my second attempt, I rewinded all the way to turn 2, and made some changes so that I can more efficiently build up attack while defending, focusing on killing their Shredders (which they went for again). It seemed good, but after killing all their Shredders and after the dust had settled, I had a bit too low econ (9 Drones). Not enough to even buy 1 Polywall per turn. Let's go back and see if we can do better! What should we change this time?|| --> ||Okay, what about not going for a 3 engi opener, and seeing if they still go for Shredders anyway? ... They only went for 1 Shredder on my next attempt. I reset at Turn 15. Onto the next attempt!|| --> ||I went back and saw that I bought a forge but floated the blue next turn due to my opponent's play. I explored delaying the forge. ... And... that's victory! In the follow spoilers I can give you a few details of what types of mistakes the AI made when I played this line, and can give you hints as to what my line was.|| -->

messages.author: spiritfryer
messages.content:
Phase 5: ||Explaining My Solution||
||The AI made only 1 Shredder again, as in an earlier attempt, but this time after fixing our "too early Forge" inefficiency, we had enough tempo going that the AI just... stopped ever absorbing for some reason?! It had 1 blue and no Walls, and was making 1 Polywall per turn. Perhaps it figured that absorbing is bad because it had a number of frontline units (Wild Drones)? Either way, this is GREAT news for us, because we are up by +2 WHOLE ABSORB per turn. That is an insane advantage. Is it enough? ... Okay after a few turns the AI starts holding Steelsplitters, so they have absorb now, but with the tempo advantage we managed to buy out ALL 10 TARSIERS! At this point I had enough momentum to coast to victory, with a few minor tactical adjustments here or there.|| --> ||"Okay... but so what was the line?" you might ask. Well, as mentioned earlier I initially explored 3-engi openers, and then switched to a 2-engi opener. I observed it always opening with an early Forge on 2 engis, and that remained true throughout all my attempts today. In order to exploit Shredder-killing ASAP, I open with a turn 3 Animus, and make 1 Auric (+1 Drone) instead of 2 Drones, to keep my turns flexible.|| --> ||"What then?" Glad you asked. I pump Tarsiers whenever I can. In previous attempts I opened quick forge for a Wall, but as I mentioned, at some point that felt inefficient and I even floated an early blue. So the winning fix was to delay the forge by a turn, and use 1 early Rhino for defense in the meantime.|| --> ||"And?" Well, from there it was a matter of sharp tactical play, prioritizing Tarsiers while defending well, exploiting oppo defenses and minimizing their exploits on us. I had 12 Drones, often +1 Auric, and sometimes Wild Drones that the AI often killed very soon.|| --> ||I realize that this has been more of a diary than an explicit guide, but I hope you have found it helpful! Godspeed, and let me know if you still end up stuck!||
